title: Karine Bengualid -Hiring Manager Interview - Andi | Technical Managing Editor
meeting_title: Karine Bengualid -Hiring Manager Interview - Andi | Technical Managing Editor

transcript.text: How are you?
This meeting is being recorded.
Well, happy Monday.
Thank you for being agreeable to first thing Monday morning, or at least for me.
I don't know.
Where are you located?
I'm on Atlantic time, so I'm on the Atlantic coast.
So it's very early for you, but it's not so early.
It's 10.15 for me.
Okay, not too bad.
Great.
No, not at all.
Great.
Wait, so on the Atlantic coast, where does that put you?
In Moncton, New Brunswick.
Oh, amazing.
Yeah, just east of Maine.
Oh, perfect.
Cool.
Well, I will give you a little bit of context about myself and GrowthX and kind of how I'm thinking about this role, just to kick us off.
And then I'll ask you some questions about yourself, your experience, and then I'll try and leave.
I like to leave.
I'm like...
Like five, ten minutes at the end for questions.
So yeah, that's the way that will go.
And I'm just going to call this out that I have like multiple drinks in front of me because I'm still organizing my morning.
So just, it's not water.
worries.
Okay, cool.
Well, like I said, thanks for scheduling some time.
I'm really excited to meet you, especially knowing that you've worked with some of our colleagues before.

So I lead customer operations at GrowthX, which just means that I help kind of like manage the processes that, and the organization of our pods as that are going into kind of our content production process.
So right now, GrowthX operates in a pod system managing our accounts.
So we have content managers.
This role is, I think like there are.
great.
That's hard.
Dan
two tracks that are really important for it.
One is like project management and coordination.
So that also means coordination with our clients and internal stakeholders.

And then the other side is more technical coordination with our engineering team, because everything that we do is built off of an internal platform that leverages AI workflows to kind of customize and generate output at scale.
So the platform that we use today has been live for seven weeks.
So it's still brand new.
We used to use air apps and then decided that we wanted to kind of have our own ability to customize.
So that means that there are still some kinks to work out.
And we are looking for someone who can help name workshop.
And are
Kind of express specifically like what they want to be better when our workflows aren't putting out the right content.
So not only a tastemaker, but somebody that can also really like identify like where they think the process is going wrong.
And that does require just a little bit of technical and AI know how.
So those are the two pieces that like we're really thinking about when we're asking questions for this role.
And yeah, and then the third person in the pod, which I didn't mention is a director role.

So or strategic account manager, and that's the person that's really like, really customer facing and helps just like set the long term strategy and roadmap with the client for what we'll do when we pivot, helping set metrics and things like that.
So any questions on that, like overview?
Yes.
So, you mentioned the third person, so these are two different people, like the project management coordination, and then the technical coordination with the engineering team?
No, so that's you.
Okay.
So, yeah, the technical managing editor, I think, like, wears two hats is kind of why.
So, the content manager is the person that day-to-day is running those workflows.
Gotcha.

So, you will, like, have kind of editorial say over, like, when something's ready to go out, and, you know, be project managing kind of what those content managers are focused on, though you won't be directly managing them.
And then just, like, managing for the client, and then the other side would be, like, that technical back and forth.
Okay.
Perfect.
Got it.
Thank you for clarifying.
Awesome.
Um, well, why don't you tell me a little bit about yourself now?
Sure.
Lots to tell.
So I started my career off as a marketing generalist.
And when I moved out west, I started freelancing and specialized and ended up falling into the specialty of content marketing and specifically for B2B SaaS and e-commerce brands.

And so I've always found that my previous like marketing generalist experience, like I was a manager director level when I started freelancing, has helped me because as a freelancer in content marketing, because I can do the marketing strategy and I understand that part of the business, excuse me.
And so it's just really helped to inform all the content marketing that I do and understand why.
My clients need to do X, Y, and Z or, you know, whatever the case may be.
So that's been pretty cool.
Yeah, and so I've worn a bunch of different hats as a freelancer, specifically in editing, strategy, writing, content management, basically from start to finish.
And, yeah, I'm excited to speak with you today.
Awesome.
Well, let's start kind of maybe with some of the generalist work or in your experience.
How have you, like, shaped client relationships or what has been your role in client-facing work to date?
Sorry, can you kind of elaborate on that, please?
Yeah, so as I mentioned, one aspect of this role is project management, and that means interacting with clients on.
Timelines.
And so I'm just trying to get a sense of your comfort in speaking with clients.

And then, you know, I'm assuming that in some of these content roles, and even in your generalist role, if you were ever client facing, kind of how you've structured your work with your team, and also like where you fit in working with clients.
Yeah, totally.
Yeah, I mean, I'll just stick to the freelance stuff, because I think that's more relevant.
But yeah, absolutely.
I mean, I'm always client facing with all of my clients.
Yeah, that makes sense.
And yeah, so I mean, you know, having weekly, biweekly, monthly calls, however, it is like one on one, just to do status updates and see where everything is up to par.
Having.
And just that relationship.
Sometimes, depending on the size of the company that I'm working with, if it's like a much bigger company, then there'll be like a group call once in a while to update everybody kind of thing.
Or see that all the different pieces, because I'm like one wheel in the cog doing the content, like there's still stuff to do on their end.
So we kind of just always connect.
And yeah, I mean, it's just about maintaining that relationship.
And just communicating and making sure that I have all my deadlines and all my projects and asking the questions that I need to make sure everything gets done on time.
Does that?
I feel like that doesn't answer your question.
That's okay.
can.
I mean, it does in a way.
Tell me about like, how many clients are you managing at a time?
Or have you managed?
And if it's multiple, how you think about?
Yeah, so I mean, I've managed anywhere from one to two clients at a time all the way to like maybe five to seven.
And so the way that I manage prioritization is just having all of my tools like set up.
And so for instance, like whether if the client as a freelancer, typically I find that companies have their own like project management software.
So I've used things like Airtable, ClickUp, Monday, Trello, Asana, like all of those kind of PM tools.
But if they don't have that to kind of track projects, and I have my own.
And so I usually like to just put everything in there based on the client.
And like,
The stage of the project, the delivery date, you know, all of that kind of stuff.
So that I can have everything kind of at a quick glance.
I can look at it from, like, my calendar view to see, like, which deadlines are coming up sooner rather than, you know, others.
And so it just helps me kind of have, like, you know, a very big overview, but also I can, like, kind of drill down.
And so typically I try to, you know, manage my deadlines in terms of looking at the priority and assessing, like, how long something's going to take me ahead of time to deliver.
And then just kind of mapping out all, you know, the different steps.
And so it's important to stay organized for that, especially when you have more than, you know, just a couple of clients.
Because things can kind of fall.
Well, under quickly, but yeah, so I just, I have a great tech stack, and I'm usually able to use whatever they have.
And like I said, if they don't have it, then grab my tools and just get them using it.
And what like AI tools or other like tools or do you prefer to use either in your like content development work or for project management or whatever?
Yeah, I mean, I, there's so many that I've used and that I like to use.
So it just really depends.
But in terms of AI, I think it really just depends on getting your bearings and knowing the right questions to ask.
So I've gotten really good with a few of them and, you know, chat.
PPT has gotten a lot of slack, but I think that playing with it for so many years now, like I've gotten pretty good at, you know, knowing what prompts and stuff to ask.
And I also use like Claude and Gemini, Deep Seek.
One of my clients was Unbounce.
And so I use their Smart Copy, which was cool.
And of course, like, you know, MS, Image Creator, and a bunch of others.
In terms of tech stack, like I said, there's Monday, Trello, Airtable, all of those things.
I've used like Slack for communications and Teams, depending on, you know, what they use.
Or I have G Suite, so, you know, Google Meet, if we need.
I've used ClearScope and Ahrefs, SimilarWeb, Hotjar.
Writing, really like Grammarly and Hemingway.
Google Analytics, which I haven't used in a little while, but I mean, it's pretty straightforward.
Not the best, but pretty straightforward.
For CMS, obviously WordPress, Wordflow, Wix, Shopify, Kajabi.
I use HTML cleaner just to make sure everything is clean, as the name suggests, a few CRMs and yeah, so.
Yeah, let's go back specifically to AI.
So where does AI come into your workflow normally?
And if you want to talk me through like your full content development process, that's fine as well.
Sure.
So I've been really into AI.
I really enjoy using it.
I it.
So specifically to like different stages of the content creation.
So sometimes I'll use it to just come up with ideas.
So ideas for headlines, for subheads, for topics, if I have specific keywords, but I'm looking, you know, for, for like a different angle.
Um, I'll use it to kind of, you know, brainstorm ideas.
Like I use it more of like a sounding board, if that makes sense.
Um, and just trying to kind of piece like everything together.
So I usually like, I'll do my research first, and then I'll use it to be like, okay, this is the information I have.
Um, can you, and these are the keywords, for instance, that I need to rank for, can you come up with, you know, 10 to 15, or, you know, fewer?
Um,
The topics that we can write about, and just kind of go from there.
And so, once I do that, I just kind of continue to, sorry, I just lost my screen.
Oh, there you are.
Hi.
Yeah, so once I have some ideas, like, I never use the output, which I think is a problem a lot of people have, because ChatGPT is still not great.
And it's a very, like, generalist tool.
But I love it for the, like I said, the brainstorming side of it.
So once I get those outputs, then I'll just kind of piece it all together.
And I usually ask for so many pieces, because I like to get the variety.
And, you know, I might use, like, one word from everything that they've given me, and just put it all together.
And then from there, I'll just start to craft the article or the content, depending on whatever it is.
And then at the end, you know, sometimes if I'm struggling, you know, with putting in a conclusion or doing something like that, like I'll use it again.
Or if I'm not loving some of my, you know, H2s or whatever the case may be, I'll do that.
Yeah.
And then I always, sorry, go ahead.
No, that's okay.
I'm curious how you used it specifically in the exercise then.
You know, those are a lot of like very broad examples, but, you know, talk to me about how you approach the exercise with AI in mind.
Yeah.
Let me just pull it up.
Sorry, I should have had it.
All right, my nerves are all over the place.
Yeah, it's okay.
Yeah, so for the assignment, I chose Engine as the company.
And so what I kind of did was like all of my research and I did all of my research on the brand, on the website, like read the article.
Like, kind of got familiar with what they did.
And from there, I just kind of wrote, like, the different steps.
So, like, the product approach, the market positioning, the competitive landscape, and then the strategic direction.
Like, all that stuff was really easy.
It was, like, on their website, easy to grab.
What I used it for was I picked out, because it can't skim, like, whole websites and all of that, I picked out specific examples of content.
So they had a bunch of different types of content, like case studies and guides and articles.
And so I picked out a bunch from each category, and I fed it to them.
And I asked it to kind of come up with...
And the, like, brand guidelines that it saw.
So I had noticed already some things, but just to make sure, like, whatever it came up with, like, I kind of checked on the website to make sure it was correct.
So, you know, things like being sentence case and whatever.
So it helped me kind of create the brand voice and the framework.
And then, you know, the do's and don'ts.
In terms of the personas, it was also pretty easy with them because they already had their personas listed.
So I know the assignment kind of said only pick three personas, but they had their four, like, right there.
So I was like, I can't leave one out.
So it was kind of easy to pick that out.
So I kind of helped it along.
So I said, you know, like, these are the four personas.
Personas, here's the webpages, like talk to me about who they are, like all of the different aspects.

So it kind of gave me that info and I just kind of collated it with the information that I had gotten as well from the website and kind of reworded it and made sure that it was like, you know, accurate.
Which tools did you use?
I used ChatTBT.

And yeah, and then in terms of rewriting the article, same kind of idea, like I just kind of read, I tried to grab something that was a little bit older that I thought, you know, might not have been revised in a while.
And I kind of gave it like a different, excuse me, excuse me.
You okay?
Yeah, just a frog in my throat.
Yeah, so I kind of went through it and thought, okay, like, how can we kind of give it a different little twist?
Yeah, and so I just looked it up and kind of shared the article with AI and said, like, what different angles, like, give me three to five different angles that we could work on for this piece.
What would, what do you suggest would be different?
What do you suggest we keep?
Like, what's, you know, kind of thing?
And then, yeah, the other thing that I tried to do was also weave in some internal links, but as well some external links to help.
Support the article, and just get it some more links.
I didn't think it had enough.
And, yeah, so that's kind of how I did it.
Awesome.
Well, I want to make sure that there's time for you to ask me some questions.
So I'll pause here and just say what questions you have for me.
Sure.
I have a lot.

So, yeah, so Andy, from your perspective, I'd love to know, like, I know you said this part of this role is a lot of technical coordination with the engineering team to make sure that, you know, every, like all the little nooks and crannies kind of get fixed.
to make sure that the outputs are better.
So what kind of, I guess,
Kind of other operational bottlenecks, if you will.
Do you see this position kind of being able to help with?
Yeah, well, the workflows are, I think, would be the main focus here and helping to improve those.
There's also like a really high expectation of output here.
So most managing editors are managing three to five clients.
And their teams, like across those clients, are generating 50 plus pieces of content a week.
So that's, and that should all be through workflows.
If it's not, then like you really lose a ton of time.
So, you know, like the exercise through, when done through AI should take less than an hour.
Just because of like pulling in all the different pieces.
So This
And filtering it through AI.
So yeah, it's really about leveraging the workflows and the platform itself to make sure that we can hit the deliverables that we've set for ourselves and for the clients.
Okay, great.
Three to five sounds really manageable.
Like I thought it was five to 10.
So that's even better.
And how, or I don't, I know, it's pretty new.
said it's only like seven weeks old.
But if you haven't already, how would you handle like if there was client feedback, and it required like some sort of like workflow change?
Is that something?
Yeah, so the managing editor would put in a ticket in linear, which is what we use to ask for the workflow change.
So we have like,
Human-in-the-loop processes in the, within the workflow, so you, you know, you can see the different steps and adding more context or things like that is usually what's required.

But what we're often finding is that if you make the artifacts really good that are going into those workflows, then that's as long as we're leveraging those in the workflows in the proper way, we have the right information because context is king.
Totally.
Yeah.
The, the briefs are always super important to, to getting the best outputs.
I know I've been a little bit nervous during the call this morning.
So before I let you go, I was wondering if there's anything else I can clarify about my experience.
Um, yeah, I mean, I think, like, I, I would love to, like, I would have loved to hear.
A little bit more about, like, specifics of when you used AI.
So that was why I asked about, like, how you developed the article.
And then, like, I think just generally, I was missing specific examples throughout the conversation.

So, yeah, I mean, I guess, like, if you want to try and answer, like, the thing that I don't really have a good sense of is, like, an example of when you were managing multiple clients and pushing a project forward.
Yeah, sure.
So, let me just pull up an example.
Yeah, so, I mean, I always have several, like I said, one to two to three, five to seven clients on the, go.
And so, one of my...
Clients.
I had a really big project.
It was Talkspace.
And they are a mental health online service provider.
And they wanted to launch their B2B blog.
They already had a B2C, but they really wanted to target B2B businesses, and they didn't have a specific blog for that.
So aside from all of my other work, that was like a really big project that I had to kind of undertake in addition to everything else.
So it was a lot of meetings, not only with the key contact at Talkspace, who was the senior B2B marketing manager, but also like their account managers, their design group, their SMEs as well.
To kind of make sure everybody was on the same page.
So my responsibility was to kind of...skorgRISKASER.

Come up with a strategy and the types of articles, but in order to do that, it was kind of informed by all of those meetings, by meeting the experts and seeing what they needed when they go out to HR teams, what kind of content they're looking for and what kind of content can support them, what guides versus articles, that kind of thing.

And so it was all about having all of those meetings, so a lot, like you had asked me in the beginning, a lot of client-facing stuff, and just being able to understand all of kind of the pain points that they experience throughout their example journey, and then coming up with a strategy on, okay, this is who Talkspace is, and this is who they are when they're B2B, understanding who their key audience members are, and what information
Attraction them the most, and, you know, pitching different types of articles and gated content, and sales pieces for that.
So once all of those meetings were done, I created the strategy, then we met again, and I pitched what I was suggesting, and everybody approved it, and then the work started.
And so I started creating the work, a lot of those was independent research that I did.
But some of it was also interviewing their SMEs, they have counselors on their team, things like that.
So I was able to meet with them and specifically ask questions, get them to review the information that I have.
And they, yeah, and so they were able to help me, you know, with the chunks that I was missing.
And then,
And once all of that content kind of went through editing and graphic design and all of that, it went on the website and we were able to launch.
And last I checked, it was garnering quite a bit of traction.
I believe it was around $70,000 per month.
And the sales pieces were doing really well as well, like really answering most of those key questions that potential clients had in the beginning.
So helping the sales and the accounts team.
Awesome.
That was helpful.
Thank you.
Well, we're over time, but thanks for everything and giving me an extra example.
And yeah, thanks so much for your time and I'll talk to you soon.
Okay.
Thanks, Andi.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Hiring manager interview with Karine Bengualid for the Technical Managing Editor role at GrowthX.](https://fathom.video/share/-jt431XuxxtvYkHCoiY5Mc4jkoFK22u1?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Role combines project management/client coordination and technical coordination with engineering team](https://fathom.video/share/-jt431XuxxtvYkHCoiY5Mc4jkoFK22u1?tab=summary&timestamp=4.0)
  - [GrowthX uses AI-driven platform (7 weeks old) for content production; role involves improving workflows](https://fathom.video/share/-jt431XuxxtvYkHCoiY5Mc4jkoFK22u1?tab=summary&timestamp=154.0)
  - [Karine has extensive freelance content marketing experience, especially in B2B SaaS and e-commerce](https://fathom.video/share/-jt431XuxxtvYkHCoiY5Mc4jkoFK22u1?tab=summary&timestamp=310.0)
  - [High output expectation: 3-5 clients, 50+ content pieces/week through AI-powered workflows](https://fathom.video/share/-jt431XuxxtvYkHCoiY5Mc4jkoFK22u1?tab=summary&timestamp=1404.0)

## Topics

### Role Overview and Expectations

  - [Two key aspects: project management/coordination and technical coordination with engineering](https://fathom.video/share/-jt431XuxxtvYkHCoiY5Mc4jkoFK22u1?tab=summary&timestamp=91.0)
  - [Part of a pod system: Content Manager, Technical Managing Editor, Director/Strategic Account Manager](https://fathom.video/share/-jt431XuxxtvYkHCoiY5Mc4jkoFK22u1?tab=summary&timestamp=209.0)
  - [Manage content workflows, provide editorial oversight, and improve AI-driven processes](https://fathom.video/share/-jt431XuxxtvYkHCoiY5Mc4jkoFK22u1?tab=summary&timestamp=176.0)
  - [High output: 3-5 clients, generating 50+ content pieces weekly](https://fathom.video/share/-jt431XuxxtvYkHCoiY5Mc4jkoFK22u1?tab=summary&timestamp=1411.0)
  - [Expectation to complete AI-assisted exercises in under an hour](https://fathom.video/share/-jt431XuxxtvYkHCoiY5Mc4jkoFK22u1?tab=summary&timestamp=1428.0)

### Karine's Background and Experience

  - [Started as marketing generalist, specialized in B2B SaaS and e-commerce content marketing](https://fathom.video/share/-jt431XuxxtvYkHCoiY5Mc4jkoFK22u1?tab=summary&timestamp=303.0)
  - [Freelance experience: editing, strategy, writing, content management](https://fathom.video/share/-jt431XuxxtvYkHCoiY5Mc4jkoFK22u1?tab=summary&timestamp=369.0)
  - [Client management: 1-7 clients simultaneously](https://fathom.video/share/-jt431XuxxtvYkHCoiY5Mc4jkoFK22u1?tab=summary&timestamp=542.0)
  - [Project management tools: Airtable, ClickUp, Monday, Trello, Asana](https://fathom.video/share/-jt431XuxxtvYkHCoiY5Mc4jkoFK22u1?tab=summary&timestamp=573.0)
  - [AI tools: ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, DeepSeek, Unbounce Smart Copy](https://fathom.video/share/-jt431XuxxtvYkHCoiY5Mc4jkoFK22u1?tab=summary&timestamp=713.0)
  - [Other tools: ClearScope, Ahrefs, SimilarWeb, Hotjar, Grammarly, Hemingway, Google Analytics](https://fathom.video/share/-jt431XuxxtvYkHCoiY5Mc4jkoFK22u1?tab=summary&timestamp=774.0)

### AI Integration in Workflow

  - [Uses AI for ideation: headlines, subheads, topics, keyword angles](https://fathom.video/share/-jt431XuxxtvYkHCoiY5Mc4jkoFK22u1?tab=summary&timestamp=846.0)
  - [Research first, then AI as a "sounding board" for brainstorming](https://fathom.video/share/-jt431XuxxtvYkHCoiY5Mc4jkoFK22u1?tab=summary&timestamp=869.0)
  - [Doesn't use direct AI output; combines ideas from multiple suggestions](https://fathom.video/share/-jt431XuxxtvYkHCoiY5Mc4jkoFK22u1?tab=summary&timestamp=923.0)
  - [Used ChatGPT for the assignment: brand voice, personas, article rewriting](https://fathom.video/share/-jt431XuxxtvYkHCoiY5Mc4jkoFK22u1?tab=summary&timestamp=1007.0)

### Client Project Example: Talkspace B2B Blog Launch

  - [Coordinated with multiple stakeholders: marketing manager, account managers, design group, SMEs](https://fathom.video/share/-jt431XuxxtvYkHCoiY5Mc4jkoFK22u1?tab=summary&timestamp=1653.0)
  - [Developed strategy based on stakeholder input and pain points](https://fathom.video/share/-jt431XuxxtvYkHCoiY5Mc4jkoFK22u1?tab=summary&timestamp=1708.0)
  - [Created content strategy, pitched ideas, conducted interviews with SMEs](https://fathom.video/share/-jt431XuxxtvYkHCoiY5Mc4jkoFK22u1?tab=summary&timestamp=1778.0)
  - [Resulted in $70,000 monthly traction and improved sales support](https://fathom.video/share/-jt431XuxxtvYkHCoiY5Mc4jkoFK22u1?tab=summary&timestamp=1816.0)

## Next Steps

  - Andi to review Karine's qualifications and experience against role requirements
  - Evaluate Karine's ability to manage high-volume content production through AI workflows
  - Consider Karine's client management and technical coordination capabilities
  - Assess Karine's potential to improve existing AI-driven content processes
